title: AP U.S. History DBQ Practice Exam 09

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which urban political machines shaped city government and residents’ lives in the United States from 1870 to 1900.
Document 1
Author/Origin: Described ward map created for this practice exam, large northeastern city, circa 1885.
Source Type: Described map.
The map shows several crowded working-class wards near docks, factories, rail yards, and tenement districts. Symbols mark ward club offices, firehouses, police stations, public works projects, and polling places. The wards with the most club offices also show high voter turnout and frequent requests for street repairs, coal assistance, and public jobs.
Sourcing Cues: The map emphasizes the local, neighborhood-based structure of machine politics and its connection to services, jobs, and elections.
Document 2
Author/Origin: Original simulated note from a ward captain to a precinct worker, New York City, 1882; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Political organization note.
“Mrs. Donnelly’s rent trouble should be taken to the alderman before Friday. Send two men to help the Rossi family with coal. Remind the new voters on Baxter Street where to register. A man remembers who helped him when winter came, and he remembers again on election day.”
Sourcing Cues: The note reflects the practical, personal, and transactional nature of ward-level machine politics.
Document 3
Author/Origin: Original simulated statement from a municipal reform association, Philadelphia, 1887; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Reform statement excerpt.
“Contracts for paving, lighting, and water service are awarded not to the most efficient bidder but to the loyal friend of the organization. Taxpayers pay twice: once in money wasted and again in government degraded. The city must replace partisan favoritism with professional administration.”
Sourcing Cues: The statement reflects middle-class reformers’ criticism of corruption, patronage, and inefficiency.
Document 4
Author/Origin: Original simulated letter from a recently naturalized voter to his brother, Chicago, 1890; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Primary text source.
“The ward men know every family on the block. They find work for some, translate papers for others, and speak to police when trouble comes. The reform gentlemen say we are bought, but they do not come when a child is sick or a landlord threatens. I vote for those who know my street.”
Sourcing Cues: The letter reflects the viewpoint of an urban working-class voter who values machine services despite reform criticism.
Document 5
Author/Origin: Described political cartoon from a reform newspaper, United States, 1894.
Source Type: Described political cartoon.
The cartoon shows a large figure labeled “The Boss” sitting behind a city council desk. Strings run from his fingers to small figures labeled “alderman,” “contractor,” “police chief,” and “building inspector.” Outside the window, citizens wait in line at a broken water pump while a sign reads “Taxes Paid Here.”
Sourcing Cues: The cartoon reflects reformers’ claim that political bosses controlled public offices and diverted city resources.
Document 6
Author/Origin: Municipal budget table created for this practice exam, selected city departments, 1875–1895.
Source Type: Data table.
The table is titled “Selected Urban Spending Patterns in One Large City.”
Department | 1875 spending trend | 1895 spending trend | Common controversy
Street cleaning | low and irregular | higher but uneven | contracts tied to party allies
Police | expanding | expanded further | political influence over appointments
Public works | growing | much larger | patronage jobs and contract favoritism
Charity relief | informal and limited | more connected to ward networks | aid exchanged for loyalty
A note beneath the table states: “Urban growth increased demand for services, but machine control raised questions about corruption, favoritism, and access.”
Sourcing Cues: The table highlights the relationship among urban growth, public services, patronage, and political controversy.
Document 7
Author/Origin: Original simulated statement from a Black church leader to a city council committee, northern city, 1896; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Public testimony excerpt.
“Our neighborhood is asked for votes, but our streets receive repairs last. Party men promise protection, yet colored workers are passed over for many city jobs. If ward politics is to speak for the people, then it must speak for all of them, not only for those whose support is easiest to reward.”
Sourcing Cues: The testimony reflects African American urban residents’ criticism of unequal access to machine benefits and municipal services.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point for a historically defensible claim evaluating the extent to which urban political machines shaped city government and residents’ lives from 1870 to 1900. The thesis must make an argument, such as that machines strongly shaped urban politics by providing services, jobs, and voter mobilization while also encouraging corruption, unequal access, and reform opposition.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing broader relevant context, such as rapid urbanization, industrialization, immigration, growth of tenements, weak municipal services, patronage politics, expansion of voting, Gilded Age corruption, or middle-class reform movements.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point for accurately using content from at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points for using content from at least six documents to support a coherent argument about ward organization, social services, voter mobilization, patronage, corruption, municipal spending, reform criticism, or unequal access to machine benefits.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point for using at least one specific, relevant piece of outside evidence not found in the documents, such as Tammany Hall, Boss Tweed, Thomas Nast, patronage, civil service reform, Pendleton Civil Service Act, tenement districts, settlement houses, municipal reform leagues, or ethnic political clubs.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. For example, a student might analyze the ward captain’s electoral motives, the reform association’s middle-class efficiency concerns, the working-class voter’s dependence on local aid, the cartoon’s reform purpose, the budget table’s link between urban growth and patronage, or the Black church leader’s criticism of unequal access.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a nuanced argument, such as explaining that machines were both corrupt and responsive; distinguishing between middle-class reform views and working-class voter needs; showing that machine benefits were uneven by race, ethnicity, gender, and neighborhood; or explaining that urban growth created real service needs that machines exploited and partially met.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ would likely argue that political machines deeply shaped city government by organizing votes, distributing jobs and aid, influencing police and public works, and controlling contracts. However, the best responses should avoid a one-sided argument. Machines could provide practical assistance to residents ignored by formal institutions, but they also encouraged corruption, favoritism, and unequal access. Useful document groupings include ward-level services, reform criticism, voter loyalty, boss control, public spending, and unequal racial access to city benefits. Relevant outside evidence could include Tammany Hall, Boss Tweed, Thomas Nast, patronage, the Pendleton Civil Service Act, tenement politics, or municipal reform leagues.
